Order Assignment Optimization of 
Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System 
Based on Order Batching 
 
研 究 生： 邱榆 婷

     郭伯 勳 博士 
 
中華 民國 一一零 年八月 
 

i 
 
  


ii 
 
  


iii 
 
ABSTRACT 
Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS) is a parts -to-picker material 
handling system that is well known to be implemented in e-commerce warehouses. 
Robots play the role of picking up movable shelves, called pods, to th

e workstations 
and returning them to the storage area.  Although the ef ficiency of the picking 
process is improved by reducing ineffective picking activities such as traveling and 
searching, moving a robot as a means of transportation consumes too much energy. 
Thus, fulfilling more orders with fewer robots is a crucial point to optimize the 
order picking process for reducing the robot moveme

nt, which can further reduce 
the energy consumption of robots. Fewer robots means fewer pods are picked at the 
picking station.  
This study focuses on order to pod assignment under consideration of the order 
batching concept, which deals with a group of orders at the same time, in an attempt 
to minimize the number of picked pods and provide a high pile -on value.  
Furthermore, this study proposes a two -phase assignment for order to pod 
assignment, which can identify the pod status before the assignment.  
Rather than handle orders one by one,  implementing the order batching 
concept to the order assignment can reduce the number of picked pods by 12.64%, 
and increase the pile -on value from 2.87 to  5.28. The proposed two -phase 
assignment can reduce the number of picked pods by 18.97%, pile-on value can 
reach 6.37 where the throughput efficiency is still the best. 
 
Keywords: Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS), Simulation, Order to Pod 
Assignment, Order Batching, Agent-Based Modeling

1. CHAPTER 1  
INTRODUCTION 
 
1.1 Background and Motivation 
With the rise of the Internet age, people are gradually adjusting their 
consumption methods and patterns, and online shopping has be come one of the 
most popular online activities wor

ldwide . The global e -commerce market is 
expected to reach 21.8% of all retail sales worldwide by 2024 [1]. Not only the 
growth of the e -commerce market but also the biggest challenge to warehousing 
operations [2]. E-commerce involves tremendous small orders, facing uncertain and 
large customer demands such as ra

pidly changing trends and promotional activities, 
online retailers need to fulfill orders as soon as possible [3]. 
To fulfill orders quickly and efficiently, warehouses play a critical role in the 
supply chain. The internal operations of warehouses must b

e executed effectively, 
including orders received, storage, sorting, task assignment, picking, packaging, 
and shipping. Among them, order  picking operation is an important and tedious 
task. From the perspective of labor requirement, the labor cost directly related to 
the order picking operation even accounts for more than 50% of the total cost  [4]. 
It can be seen that the performance of order picking operations has an 
overwhelming impact on the operating costs of the warehouse. 
A variety of Order Picking Systems (OPS) can be distinguished in warehouses, 
a classification of OPS is including five main groups [5], which shows in Figure 
1.1:  

2 
 
 
Figure

1.1 Classification of order picking systems[5] 
Within the OPS, “ Picker-to-parts” system  is the majority  of picking systems in 
warehouses [6]. It can be considered  as the basic system for the picking activity.

However, with the increasing number of custo mer orders in the e -commerce 
warehouses, the type of OPS is transformed into “Parts-to-picker” system. In order 
to reduce the operational cost and improving their operational productivity and 
efficiency, implement automation in order picking process of warehouse operations 
could help to reach the goal. Automated material handling systems tends to hav

e 
much lower error rates than manual picking, and it improves the accuracy of 
inventory management and tracking, and they can achieve higher pick rates [7]. A 
Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS) is an automated parts-to-picker system, 
where a fleet of autonomous mobile robots move between the storage area, bring 
the movable storage units, so -called pods, to the picking stations for reducing  the 
handling mo

vement of pickers to search for inventory  [7, 8]. The innovation of 
RMFS comes from a company called Kiva Systems, which was acquired by 
Amazon Robotics in 2012 [9]. Compared to traditional methods, RMFS has the 


3 
 
flexibility necessary for handling strong demand fluctuations , which is capable to 
cope with large e-commerce warehouses with many SKUs (Stock Keeping Units).  
The RMFS uses Automated Guided Vehicle s (AGV) to bring pods, which 
contains ordered items, to the picking station, workers in the picking station pick 
items to fulfill the or

ders, then AGVs bring pods back to the storage area or bring 
them to the replenishment station to replenish SKUs on the pod. The process 
includes the order picking and replenishment. Throughput rate, unit of items goes 
through the process per unit time, is usually used to evaluate the performance of 
this system. It is crucial to optimize the order picking process, it means that collect 
more orders with less effort, for improving the throughput rate of the system. Since 
the performance of the system follows the determined decision rules, the unit 
throughput rate in this system is greatly affected by the decision rules of picking 
order assignment (POA) [7], which is the assignment for selecting p ick orders to 
the picking station [10]. 
Fulfilling more orders with less number of robots, i.e.

less number of pods to  
be picked is one of the crucial issues for optimizing the order picking process in 
RMFS. In order to effectively organize the picking operations, order batching, i.e.

grouping multiple customer orders into picking orders, has been proven to be 
pivotal [11]. The order batching method is used for traditional manual order picking 
systems, picker-to-parts system, where the order picker walks or r ides (e.g. on an 
AGV) through the aisle to pick the items in the warehouse, a set of orders are split 
into several subsets of order items and assigned to the pickers, for reducing the 
picking and travel

ing time.  
However, in RMFS, one robot can only carry a single pod, and it is not feasible 
to carry all the order items in that pod at one time. Therefore, if as more as order 

4 
 
items are picked from one pod, it can be inferred that the number of picked pods in 
the picking station can be reduced, as well as the  robot movement distance. The 
average number of order items picked from one pod, named pile-on, is the most 
important indicator for reducing order picking time. The higher pile -on, the fewer 
pods are required to be transferred between the picking station a nd storage area, 
which can reduce the number of robots required [7]. Instead of handle orders one 
by one, applying the order batching concept to RMFS is a more efficient way for 
the order assignment, which can increase the pile-on value, and reduce the number 
of pods be used. Therefore, the ord er batching concept wo

uld be applied in this 
research. 
Meanwhile, different from previous research, the pick order assignment in this 
study is the assignment for selecting pods to carry the order to the picking station, 
which will be described as an order -to-pod assignment in the following content. 
This difference is based on the consideration that the first destination of the robot 
is the pod, and the picking station is the second destination. When assigning orders 
to pods, the status of pods should be considered, that is, when the assignment occurs, 
the candidate pods are moving on their way to the picking station or are still in the 
storage area and haven’t been assigned to any orders. This study proposes an order-
to-pod assignment in which the system can  identify the pods are assigned or not 
before orders assign to pods, and first consolidate the orders to the assigned pods, 
then assign the rest of the orders in the backlog to the unassigned pods. This order-
to-pod assignment process is called a two-phase assignment, which also applies the 
order batching concept, and the objective is to minimize the total number of picked 
pods. 
In this study, the order -to-pod assignment applying order batching is 

5 
 
implemented in RMFS with different scenarios, which are batc h assignment and 
two-phase assignment. The simulation platform is used as a tool

to simulate the 
overall operation of RMFS in the warehouse. Overall, this study aims to optimize 
the order picking process through the order-to-pod assignment, with minimizing the 
number of picked pods, thereby increase the pile-on value. 
 
1.2 Objective and Limitation 
This study focuses on the order -to-pod assignment process in the RMFS, 
which aims to minimize the number of picked pods at the picking station, thereby 
increase the p ile-on value a

nd reduce  the number of robots req uired and reduce 
energy consumption. Without order batching, the order is assigned in a first come 
first serve (FCFS) manner one by one, meaning that it is assigned to the pod 
immediately when it arrives at the system. Through the order batching assignment, 
the orders are grouped into a batch at a certain number of orders arrive, and then 
they will enter the assignment process together to make the order -to-pod decision 
at the same time. In the proposed two -phase assignment, parts of the orders in the 
backlog will be released to the pods which are already assigned to other orders in 
advance, and then the rest of the orders will be assigned to the unassigned pods, in 
an attempt to minimize the number of picked pods and reduce the robot movement, 
which can infer to reduce the energy consumption. The optimization objective is to 
minimize the number of picked pods. The proposed scenario

s that applying the 
order batching concept are described in detail further in Chapter 3. 
The limitation of this study is that the battery and charging process of robots 
have not been considered. The acceleration and deceleration of robots are also not 
considered in this study, it only has a constant speed. 

6 
 
1.3 Organization of the Thesis 
This study is structured as follow s. Chapter 1 presents the background and 
motivation of the study, as well as research objectives and the limitations. Chapter 
2 provides literature review. Chapter 3 describes the process, fo

Chapter 4 shows the results of different scenarios. Chapter 5 concludes 
this study.

7 
 
2. CHAPTER 2 
LITERATURE REVIEW 
 
2.1 Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS) 
Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Sys tem (RMFS) is an automated parts -to-picker 
material handling system conceived to efficiently fulfill e -commerce orders. The 
system consists of pods, robots, picking stations and replenishment stations, which 
are all movable, identical elements, and orders represent the input that enter the 
system. The cooperation of multiple robots and the coordination of many other 
resources increase the complexity. 
The process of RMFS starts when orders arrive at the system.

Each picking 
station can handle several unfinished orders. If there are remaining capacity at one 
or multiple picking stations, the order is assigned to a picking station, otherwise, it 
is added to a pool of unassigned or

The pod contains multiple items. The 
system will search the pods containing items that are required by orders and ask the 
robot to pick the pod. The robot will bring it to the picking station, and the picker 
picks the items from the pod and thus fulfills order lines from the unfinished orders 
at the picking station.  After the pick er is done with a pod, the pod is transported 
back to the empty location in the storage area. Pods in which items quantity reaches 
certain levels will trigger the system to assign a robot to bring that pod into the 
replenishment station [12]. After the replenishment process is finished, the robot 
will bring the pod back to the warehouse.  The full process is shown in Figure 2.1, 
arrows indicate the movement of the robot and pod [7]. 

8 
 
 
Figure 2.1 Order Picking and Replenishment Process of RMFS[7] 
The decision-making issues

in RMFS are interr elated. When the robot brings 
the pod back to the storage area, the pod can be located anywhere in the warehouse, 
there are several empty locations for the options. A pod typically contains multiple 
SKUs simultaneously. Different types of items may be in t he same pod, and the 
same type of items may also be located in different pods or scattered throughout the 
warehouse [13]. The system needs to make the decisions such as the location for 
returning pod and the pod sh

ould be assigned when orders arrive. Those decision -
making problems are classified as operation al problems that need to be made in 
real-time (Figure 2.2). 


9 
 
 
Figure 2.2 Hierarchical Overview of the Decision Problems in RMFS[7] 
A research analyze optimization performance by studying important decision 
rules, compare multiple rules and find correlation between them to increase order 
item throughput [7]. In an RMFS environment, the decision problems are used in 
the decision-making steps, including [14]: 
 Order Assignment (orders to stations)  
- Pick Order Assignment (POA): assignment of pick orders to pick 
stations 
- Replenishment Order Assignment (ROA): assignment of replenishment 
orders to replenishment stations 


10 
 
 Task Creation (for pods) 
- Pick Pod Selection (PPS): selection of the pods to use for picking the 
pick orders assigned at a pick station 
- Replenishment Pod Selection (RPS): selection of the pod to store one 
replenishment order 
 Task Allocation (TA) (for robots) : assignment of tasks from Tas k Creation 
and additional support tasks like idling to robots 
 Path Planning (PP) (for robots) : planning of the paths for the robots to 
execute 
The assignment of pick orders to the picking stations (Pick Order Assignment, 
POA) is a critical issue for order picking optimization in an RMFS. POA is the 
assignment for selecting pick orders from the backlog and assigns them to the 
picking station [10]. Seven performance measures in RMFS are as follows: unit 
throughput rate, pick order throughput rate, order turnover time, distance traveled 
per robot, order offset,

the fraction of orders that are late, pile -on, and the pick 
station idle time. The result shows that the POA is the most affecting decision 
rules for the throughput rate. It improves up to twice the throughput rate [7].

Another research studied processing the orders at the picking station. The 
batching and sequencing of picking orders can decrease the needs of robots. The 
decision problem is formulated as an NP -hard problem and solved by several 
heuristic algorithms to converge to the opt

imal. The result shows that the 
algorithm decreases the number of pod visits, but only for small instances. The 
results imp ly that the provided heuristic approaches would take a significant 
amount of time for larger instances and may not be feasible for real -time 

11 
 
app

lications [15]. 
Another research provides a literature review to determine the performance 
characteristics of RMFS. The result shows that t he relationship between 
performance and RMFS operational design decisions is a preliminary finding of 
a

n ongoing study [16]. 
 
2.2 Order Batching 
In the trad itional manual picking warehouses, the order pickers depart to the 
storage area to retrieve/pick items after receiving orders (picker -to-parts system). 
When the order is large, a single order picking strategy  may be applied, i.e., one 
order is picked at a time. However, when the order is small, this kind of picking 
strategy may cause the total order picking time to become longer. Therefore, 
picking a group of orders in a single picking journey can reduce travel time  [6]. 
Order batching is t he method that grouping a set of customer orders into picking 
order. This kind of method can be classified as an NP -hard problem, which can 
improve warehouse eff

iciency by reducing operating costs [17].  
The objective of order batching is to minimize the traveling time and distance 
in the manual picking process. In terms of the availability of customer order 
information, order batching can be d istinguished into static batching and dynamic 
batching [18]. In the static case, the characteristics of each customer order, i.e., the 
requested items and the corresponding quantities, are known when the batching 
decision is taken. The issue for order batching is to decide which orders should be 
assigned to a batch. With regard to dynamic batching, order arrives stochastically 
and the in formation of order becomes available only when it has arrived, which 
means customer orders arrive at different points in time while the picking process 

12 
 
is already being executed. The problem of dynamic batching is to determine the 
batch size or batch time window to minimize the expected throughput time of the 
order. 
There are basically two criteria for batching: the proximity of pick locations 
and time windows [19]. Proximity batching means assigning orders to a batch based 
on the proximity of their storage locations. The major issue for proximity batching 
is how to estimate the proximities among orders, it implicitly assumes the rule for 
picking sequencing to visit a set of st orage locations. There are some researches 
that studied order batching problem based on proximity batching, they all aim to 
minimize the processing time [20], including through minimizing the total traveling 
time [21], traveling distance [22] [23] [24], and also the maximum lead time [25]. 
Another study focused on maximizing the total association of order batches [26], 
considering the order batching as a NP -hard problem, and solved it by heuristic 
methods, such as branch -and-bound algorithm [25], genetic algori thm [22], ant 
colony optimization [23] and t abu search [24]. With respect to time window 
batching, the orders arriving during the same time interval (fixed or variable length), 
called a time window, are gathere

d as a batch. These orders are then processed 
simultaneously in the following stages. Some of the studies consider variable time 
window order batching with stocha stic order arrivals for manual picking systems, 
which means that the number of items per batch is fixed [27] [28] [29] [30], 
modeling the problem as a batch service. For each possible picking batch size, they 
first estimate the first and second moments of service time. Then usin g these 
moments, they can find the time in a random order system. Then determine the best 
pick batch size in a straightforward manner. The results of simulation experiments 
show that this met

hod provides high accuracy. Furthermore, it is simple and can be 

13 
 
easily applied in practice [6]. 
As mentioned above, most existing publications aim to minimize the order 
picker traveling time by batching the orders. However, a large batch size results in 
a shorter average processing time for each customer order, but it also leads to a large 
average waiting time. On the other hand, the average order processing time is large 
for small batch size, whereas the average waiting time is shorter. There is a trade -
off between order batch size and waiting time (throughput time) [28]. In the manual 
order picking system, the order batch is assigned to the  picking station, and the 
batch size is determined based on the required time to pick the whole batch 
completely [30]. The objective of minimizing the order picker traveling time alone 
has limitations and may not be represent the performance of order picking [31].  
In terms of order processing time, it needs to take into accou nt the order due 
time, and the tardiness of customer orders should be involved with order batching 
instead of using the total processing time as a measure of the quality of the solution 
[32]. In order to avoid production delays , the accuracy of the delivery time is an 
essential performance indicator for w

arehouses [33]. When batching customer 
orders and routing pickers, compliance with order due dates is a critical issue [34] 
[35]. Some studies consider the order batching problem in a man -aboard system 
with minimizing the penalties and tardiness as respe ctive objectives, propose a 
heuristic that first establishes batches and then determines the release times for the 
batches [32] [36]. Another research focuses on customer response times by 
considering batching and picking operations jointly. The research observes a 
tradeoff between the necessary travel time and the length of the time period fo

r 
which the customer order stays in the system, propose a two-step heuristic to solve 
the batching and routing problem simultaneously  [37]. Most studies show that the 

14 
 
positive difference between the order due time and the batch completion time of the 
assigned order [34] [38]. Reducing the processing time and travel time  for 
constructing a batch is an important fact to reduce costs and minimize customer 
response delays. On the other hand, ignoring the due date of the order in the process 
of order  batching will cause a huge dissatisfaction in customer expectations 
regarding th

e efficient responsiveness of the company. It needs to be emphasized 
that the order due date should be regarded as an important factor in order batching 
[39]. 
Besides manual picking systems, there are some studies that discuss the order 
batching problem based on automated m

aterial handling systems, with integrating 
other factors in the smart warehouses. The storage  assignment and order batching 
problem in the RMFS are studied by [40]. The storage assignment model aims to 
decide which product to put in which pod to maximize the product similarity a

nd 
the order batching model aims to minimize the number of visits of pods. This is the 
first article that considers the number of visits of pods as an objective function. For 
solving the order batching problem, the research proposed a heuristic, where a batch 
schedule is initialized with the objective of maximizing the order association or 
minimizing order alienation and improved by v ariable neighborhood search. The 
result shows that the optimized storage assignment and optimized order batching 
can noticeably reduce the number of visits of pods. 
Since the order batching problem is regarded as one of the decision -making 
problems, it may  be an option to integrate with other order picking planning 
problems and solve it. A rese

arch provides a decision support tool that integrates 
and solves the three main operational order picking planning problems, i.e., order 
batching, picker routing, and picker scheduling. The objective is to increase order 

15 
 
picking efficiency while ensuring a high customer service level. The customer order 
due times, limited availability of order pickers, and high-level storage locations are 
considered in the planning pro blem, to ensure the applicability in practice. An 
iterated local search algorithm is introduced to solve the problem effectively and 
efficiently. The result shows that solving the integration problem can reduce the 
order picking time of a real-life case by 17% [41]. 
In order to reduce the unproductive picker working time as in traditional picker-
to-parts warehousing systems, the research creates an AGV-assisted mixed-shelves 
picking systems, which c ombined the mixed -shelves storage policy (i.e.,  items of 
the same SKU are spread over several shelves in a warehouse) with AGVs, which 
brings items from the storage area to workstations instead of human pickers. The 
research integrates the order batching a nd routing problem and solves it by the 
variable neighborhood search algorithm. In this system, the pod (shelf) is assumed 
to correspond to a customer and each batch corresponds to a vehicle, each customer 
will be assigned to exactly one vehicle. For creat ing order batch, the system will 
select order by calculating the minimum possible distance be

tween random orders. 
The result shows that the mixed -shelves storage policy is more suitable than the 
usual storage policy in AGV-assisted mixed-shelves systems (saving up to 67% on 
driving distances for AGVs) [42]. 
Another study proposed a task planning approach  for RMFS based smart 
warehouses, with the aim of utilizing system resources efficiently and adaptively. 
Developing a task conversion algorithm that generates tasks from a batch of orders 
and provides a high pile -on value, and heuristic model for assignmen t of tasks to 
robots. The research considers various system dynamics such as location and 
battery level of robots, replenishment of new stock in various pods, utilization level 

16 
 
of totes, and time elapsed after generation of order tasks. For solving the problems, 
it develops

a highly realistic simulation environment that can be considered as a 
digital twin of the real system and takes into account all realistic considerations 
such as routing, queuing, turning, energy consumption, and charging of the robots. 
To obtain insight into how to design a smart warehouse system with high efficiency, 
the research also analyzes the optimal value of order batch size and the effect of 
important system parameters such as robot count, order count, and SKU. The results 
show t hat the reduction in total completion time of orders is about 40% in a 
warehouse with 2 pick stations and 40 totes, and about 46% in a warehouse with 3 
pick stations and 60 totes [43]. 
For a comprehensive overview of the order batching problem, there are several 
studies that give a literature review for order batching operations in the framework 
of order picking process, examine several research papers and exi sting reviews 
about order batching and order picking [44], and provide a discussion about order 
batching, sequencing, and picker routing problems in order to identify research 
trends and gaps to meet real conditions of warehouses operations [45]. With regard 
to different solution approaches, other researches analyze and summarize the range 
and idea of order batching by giving a review of the differen t solution approaches 
that have been suggested in the literature [46], and provide a detailed state o f-the-
art overview of the different solution methods that have been published in the 
literature [18]. 
 
  

17 
 
3. CHAPTER 3 
METHODOLOGY 
In this chapter, the proce ss flow of the overall system is described in the first 
section. The order to pod assignment problem and its scenarios

is illustrated in the 
second section. Moreover, the system configuration of the simulation is shown in 
the third part. The performance an alysis is also included in the last part of this 
chapter. 
3.1 Process Flow 
The RMFS process flow used in this study is shown in Figure 3.1. The overall 
process consists of the events between 6 elements, including order, SKU, pod, robot, 
picking station, and replenishment station.  
The picking process starts from an order arrives at the system, until the robot 
brings the pod to the picking station to finish the order; in the replenishment process, 
the pod may need to go to replenish when

the inventory is below a certain level and 
be brought back to the storage area. The detailed explanation of each event is 
described as follows: 
 Order arrival 
As the main input of the system orders continuously enter the system 
following a s

pecific distribution of their inter-arrival time. 
 SKU to Pod assignment 
For the inventory strategy, applying the ABC rule to classify SKUs in 
the warehouse. The proportion of the number of units is 60% of class A, 
25% of class B, and 15% of class C. Different types of SKUs are mixed 
in each pod. 

18 
 
 
Figure 3.1 RMFS process flow 
 Order to Pod assignment 
After orders arrive in the system, each order will be assi

gned to the pod, 
each pod can contain multiple orders . In this study, there are three 
different scenarios to conduct the order to pod assignment, including first 
come first served (FCFS), order batching assignment, and two -phase 
assignment. The detailed scenario description will be explained in 


19 
 
Section 3.2.1. 
 Pod sequencing 
After the pod is assigned to the order, a list of selected pods will be sorted 
according to the  earliest due date of the order. The sequence of the 
selected pod indicates the pod priorities to be picked. 
 Robot to Pod assignment 
AGVs will be assigned to the selected pods based on the nearest distance 
and earliest due date. Take twice the number of available AGVs from the 
list of selected pods and the assignment is conducted between AGVs and 
the selected pods. However, the distance between AGV and the selected 
pod is calculated by Manhattan distance, and applying the Hungarian 
algorithm to obtain the assignment results 


Robot routing 
The routing policy of AGV applies Simple Routing with traffic control 
policy, which follows the previous study [47]. The traffic policy used is 
deadlock and collision  prevention. This simple rule in AGV routing 
enables it to have a minimum path without doing path planning, which 
can reduce the computational time for calculation. 
 Robot to workstation assignment (picking/ replenishment station) 
When the robot reaches the highway in the storage area before going to 
the workstation, the assignment between robot and workstation is based 
on the minimum number of AGVs that queuing to be processed. 
 Robot to storage assignm

ent 
After finishing the picking or replenishment, the robot needs to bring the 
pod back to the storage area. The system will comprehensively consider 

20 
 
all the AGVs that need to go to the empty locations in the storage area 
and all the empty posit

ions together, then apply the Hungarian algorithm 
to determine the nearest empty location from the workstation. 
 Pod to Replenish 
After the pod finishes the order picking process, it will check the pod 
inventory level. If the inventory level lower than the certain percentage, 
the pod needs to be replenished, AG V will directly bring pod to the 
replenishment station. 
3.2 Order-to-Pod Assignment Problem Formulation 
Instead of assigning the order to the picking stations, each order will be 
assigned to a pod.  Different from the previous related work, orders would be 
assigned to the picking station first, and then the required pod will be called to fulfill 
the order. However, it seems to be inefficient and will cause some restrictions if a 
picking station can only open up to three or six bins to place the ordered items, and 
when the orders need to be assigned to the picking stations, it will have the 
limitation of the number of bins for the order assignment. Instead of doing that, 
considering the flexibility of system resources [43], assuming the picking station 
can handle any order, the assignment between orders to the picking station is 
omitted, and the direct assignment for orders to pods can increase th

e possibility to 
optimize the order picking process. In this section, the scenarios of order -to-pod 
assignment and the formulation of optimization problem are illustrated in detail. 
 

21 
 
3.2.1 Sce

After the order arrives, it will be assigned to the pod immediately. The FCFS 
assignment is based on the earliest due date rule, which means that the order will 
be assigned to the pod with the earliest due date. When it comes to multiple same 
options, it will be assigned to the pod randomly. 
 
Figure 3.2 FCFS assignment flow chart 


22 
 
The second scenario is the order batching assignment, this scenario processes 
a number of orders in every assignment instead of handling orders one by one. The 
number of orders to be handled at once has a crucial role to increase pile-one value 
and in an attempt to reduce the number of picked pods as well. The flow chart of 
this scenario is

shown in Figure 3.3.  
The customer orders will arrive continuously, they will stay in the system and 
wait to be processed. After a certain number of orders arrive, they will be

sorted by 
their due dates. The batch size will be determined, and the corresponded order 
batches will be created in order. Each order batch as a group of orders will be 
ass

igned at once in an assignment. The system will search for the pods which 
contain the same items as the orders in batches and its quantity is enough for the 
order, which means the qualified pods. The orders will be assigned to the qualified 
pods, and the opti mization of the assignment is to assign them to pods with the 
minimum number. The formulation of optimization is described in the next section.

As for the batch size, since a large batch size will result in a lower throughput 
[28], it won’t have too many orders in one batch. The next step of order to pod  
assignment is the robot to pod assignment, and the number of pods that could be 
assigned would be restricted by the number of the available robots, so if the number 
of orders in one batch is fixed in the first place, it could avoid the pod may not be 
allowed to be assigned t

o robots. 
 

23 
 
 
Figure 3.3 Order batching assignment flow chart 
The third scenario is the proposed two -phase assignment. Same as the order 
batching assignment, this assignment handles a group  of orders at once. The main 
difference between order batching assignment and two -phase assignment is the 
system will identify the pod status and release parts of orders to the pods which 
have already been assigned to other orders before doing the order ba tching. The 
flow chart of the two-phase assignment is shown in Figure 3.4. 


24 
 
 
Figure 3.4 Two-phase assignment flow chart 
In order to  identify the status of pods, the sy stem will differentiate the 
availability of pods in different steps. The status of pods is classified based on it is 
available to be assigned or not, and it could be divided into 6 different statuses, 
including: 
 Assigned pod 
- Static pod: waiting to be picked by robot 


25 
 
- En-route pod: from storage area to the picking station 
- Queueing pod: waiting in the queue 
- Picking pod: picked by picker at the picking station 
 Unassigned pod 
- Idle pod: no assignment yet 
- Returning pod: after being picked, on the way back to the s torage 
area 
The pod will have different statuses in the moving processes. When the pod is in 
the storage area and hasn’t been assigned to any order, at this point it is in an “idle 
pod” status. After it is assigned to orders, the status will be turned into  “assigned 
pod” and wait to be picked by a robot, so it is classified as “static pod”. When the 
robot arrives and picks the pod, it will be brought to the picking station from the 
storage area, and the status will be “en-route pod”. After the pod reaches the picking 
station, it may need to wait for other pods to be processed in the queue, so the status 
is “queueing pod”. When the pod is picked by the picker in the picking station, the 
status will change into “picking pod” and not allow to be assigned anymor e at this 
point. After the pod is done in the picking station, the robot will bring it back to the 
empty location of the storage area, and the status will be “returning p

od”. And the 
pod will be idle again after it is put back in the storage area. The cycl e of the pod 
status is shown in Figure 3.5. 

26 
 
 
Figure 3.5 The cycle of the pod status 
As for the overall process of the two-phase assignment scenario, after the order 
arrival and sorting. As shown in Figure 3.4, in Phase 1, the system will first identify 
the pod status and search for the assigned pods. If there are some orders that match 
the assigned pods, they will be assigned to  those pods. In Phase 2, after releasing 
orders to the assigned pods, if there are some orders left, they will only be assigned 
to the unassigned pods, which are those still idle in the storage area or on the way 
for returning to the empty location.

This section introduces the tool that used in thi s study, including the system

3.3.1 System Architecture 
In this study, the system is implemented by three integrated platforms, 
including NetLogo, P

ython, and Microsoft Excel. The system architecture is shown 
in the Figure 3.6. As a simulation platform, NetLogo is used to visualize the 
warehouse operation process in RMFS. It is an agent -based modeling and 
simulation that enables agents

to communicate with each  other. Agents can 
represent real entities, such as AGVs and workstations, or virtual entities like the 
picking process and replenishment process managers. Python is a programming 
language responsible for system functions like assignment results. The syste m 
functions are built-in NetLogo, which integrate with Python to obtain feedback on

optimization results. As the database, Microsoft Excel provides inputs and outputs 
from NetLogo and Python in form of CSV files, including layout design, orders, 
settings, pods and items.

3.3.2 Simulation Layout 
The warehouse layout consists of picking stations,

storage area, and 
replenishment stations. Figure 3.7 shows the configuration of warehouse layout that 
built-in NetLogo. At the top of the layout, there are picking stations with pickers 
and queuing tracks. In the middle area, there is a storage area, including pods 
containing items. The selected pod with a different color indicates that the pod has 
been assigned to orders that will be picked by AGV later. The empty storage 
location with the blank square within pods means that AGV could bring the pod 
back to that position after the pod is picked. AGV can move in one-way aisle, and 
it is allowed to move underneath the pod without directions. In the aisle, AGV can 
only choose to move forward or stop. When it encounters an intersection, it can turn 
according to the direction of the next aisle to go. Two -lane highways are provided 
near the workstations to choose different directions for routes to or out of the 
workstations. There are two types of AGVs, which can be divided into picking 
AGVs and replenishing AGVs according to their functions. At the bottom of th e

5. CHAPTER 5 
CONCLUSION AND FUTURE RESEARCH 
5.1 Conclusion 
RMFS is a parts-to-picker system that improves the picking rate significantly 
compared to the traditional methods especially for large e -commerce warehouses 
with many SKUs. It is critical for optimizing picking order, i.e.

to collect more order 
items with less effort. Grouping multiple customer orders has been proven to be 
pivotal for an efficient way in the picking operation, with fulfilling more orders 
with less number of robots, which infer to less number of picked

pods. The previous 
related research is choosing a pick order for the picking station. In contrast, this 
study assigns orders to pods. In this study, in order to fulfill more orders with fewer 
pods and increase the pile-on value at the same time, the order batching method is 
implemented in the order -to-pod assignment, handling orders in a group at once 
instead of one by one. Furthermore, the proposed two-phase assignment is divided 
into two steps to assign orders to pods, which first assign parts of orders to the pods 
that have been assigned to other orders, and then assign the rest of orders to the 
pods that haven’t been assigned to any order in the second step. The optimization 
objective is to minimize the number of picked pods  while increasing the pile -on 
value as well. 
This study conducts three simulations with different scenarios, including the 
FCFS as a baseline, order batching assignment, and the proposed two -phase 
assignment for order -to-pod assignment.  By implementing the order batching 
method for order -to-pod assignment, it can significantly reduce the number of 
picked pods by 12.64% compared to the baseline simulation, and increase the pile-

41 
 
on value to 5.28. Moreover, the proposed two-phase assignment also includes order 
batching that can reduce more number of picked pods, which can reduce by 18.97% 
compared to the baseline, indicating that it only uses nearly half of the total number 
of pods. The pile-on value has also been increased significantly fro m the baseline 
of 2.87 to 6.37 in the two-phase assignment, which means around 6 order items be 
picked from one pod if applying two -phase assignment. Furthermore, order batch 
size may influence the throughput rate, however, in the two -phase assignment, the 
throughput efficiency doesn’t drop too much, and it even reaches 98.11% when the 
batch size is 20. Noted that the batch size could not exceed the number of available 
robots, for avoiding the decrease of throughput. The proposed two -phase 
assignment process detects the pod status before the assignment, and then obtains 
better performance with higher pile -on value and fewer number of picked pods , 
which can

reduce the robot movement and energy consumption. 
5.2 Future Research 
In future work, the multi -line order can be considered in the order batching 
strategy for optimizing the order picking process. When there are multiple SKUs 
and different quantities of order items, develop a different method to deal with 
orders for adapting the complexity is necessary. For th e simulation, the charging 
process of AGVs could be developed. The factors of AGV movement such as 
acceleration, deceleration, and turning time can also be invested for deeper analysis. 
At the strategic level of RMFS, the warehouse layout including lots of  elements, 
such as the number of workstations and their placement, pod batch size, and other

configurations, that can be adjusted and find an optimal setting.
